title: Thinking with AI
text: Human-AI-Guide · Updated Apr 2026

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.1 The Illusion of Progress
Artificial intelligence tools are becoming part of everyday work.
They can generate text, write code, summarize documents, and suggest solutions in seconds.
Because of this, many people feel more productive.
But there is a problem.
The presence of powerful tools is often mistaken for understanding.
It is now easy to produce outputs:
However, producing an output is not the same as understanding it.
And more importantly:
An output you cannot explain is not a result you can defend.

title: 2  Preface
text:
explanations

reports

analyses

even full workflows

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.2 Where Things Go Wrong
Most people start like this:

“Explain this”

“Give me ideas”

“What should I do?”

This approach hands over direction to the tool.
The result is often:

generic responses

shallow reasoning

decisions without ownership

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.3 A Different Approach
This guide is built on a simple but critical shift:

Start with thinking. Then use AI.

More specifically:

Start with a position.

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.4 What is a Position?
A position is your current thinking before using AI.
It can be:

a problem you are trying to solve

a direction you want to take

a hypothesis you want to test

Even a rough starting point is enough.
What matters is that you are not starting from zero.

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.5 The Core Principle
AI should not define your direction.
It should extend it.
This guide follows a simple reasoning loop:

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.6 Human → AI → Human

Human (You begin)

define the problem

state your position

AI (Tool support)

expand possibilities

refine structure

challenge assumptions

Human (You decide)

evaluate critically

interpret carefully

make decisions

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.7 Why This Matters
In real work:

decisions have consequences

interpretations must be justified

results must be explainable

If AI produces something you do not fully understand:

you cannot defend it

you cannot trust it

you cannot improve it

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.8 The CDI Perspective
This guide is part of the Complex Data Insights (CDI) framework.
Across CDI, the focus is not only on producing outputs, but on:

understanding what those outputs mean

evaluating their reliability

translating them into defensible decisions

Using AI does not change this responsibility.
It makes it more important.

title: 2  Preface
section: 2.9 What This Guide Will Teach You
text:
2.9 What This Guide Will Teach You
This guide does not focus on tools or tricks.
Instead, it focuses on how to:

start with a clear position

use AI to extend your thinking

interrogate outputs critically

transform outputs into defensible claims

title: 2  Preface
text:
2.10 A Final Note
AI is powerful.
But it does not replace thinking.

AI amplifies the quality of your thinking.

This guide will help you make that amplification meaningful.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.1 The Basic Definition
Many people use AI by starting with a request.
They ask for an explanation, a plan, a summary, or a solution.
But in real work, the starting point should not be a request.
It should be a position.
A position is what allows you to use AI with direction instead of dependence.
A position is your current thinking before using AI.
It is not the final answer.
It is not a polished conclusion.
It is your starting point.
A position may be:
In simple terms:

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
a problem you are trying to solve
a direction you want to take
a hypothesis you want to test
a judgment you want to refine
a workflow you want to structure



A position is the point from which your reasoning begins.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.2 Why Position Matters
Without a position, AI takes over the direction.
That often leads to:

generic outputs
shallow reasoning
weak ownership
poor decisions

When you begin with a position, AI becomes more useful.
It can help you:

sharpen what you already think
reveal what you may have missed
organize your reasoning more clearly
challenge weak assumptions

This is the difference between using AI to think and letting AI think for you.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
section: 3.3 A Position Does Not Need to Be Perfect
text:
3.3 A Position Does Not Need to Be Perfect
One reason people skip this step is that they think they need clarity before they begin.
That is not true.
A position can be rough.
It can be incomplete.
It can even be wrong.
What matters is that it exists.
For example:

“I think this model may be overfitting.”
“I want to compare these two approaches.”
“I need to explain this result more clearly.”
“I suspect this pattern is driven by data quality.”
“I want to turn this into a reproducible workflow.”

These are not final answers.
They are positions.
And that is enough to begin responsibly.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.4 Position vs Request
This distinction is central.
A request asks for output.
A position provides direction.
Compare the difference.

3.4.1 Request only

“Explain machine learning.”
“Write me a business plan.”
“Analyze this dataset.”
“Give me ideas.”

These may produce output, but they do not establish your thinking.


3.4.2 Position-based start

“I want to understand which machine learning method fits this problem and why.”
“I am developing a small consulting service and need a realistic business structure.”
“I want to assess whether variable X is associated with outcome Y.”
“I am exploring three possible directions and want help comparing them.”

The second set is stronger because the user has already entered the reasoning process.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.5 The Structure of a Position
A position does not need to be long.
Usually, it contains at least one of the following:

3.5.1 A problem
What are you trying to solve?
Example:

“I need to determine whether this result is reliable enough to report.”



3.5.2 A direction
What are you trying to do?
Example:

“I want to convert this rough analysis into a clearer workflow.”



3.5.3 A hypothesis
What do you think may be true?
Example:

“I think the difference between groups may be driven by sampling imbalance.”

A simple working formula is:

Position = Problem + Direction + (Optional) Hypothesis

This is not a strict rule.
It is a practical way to make your starting point clearer.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.6 Position Creates Better Prompts
Prompt quality is often discussed as if it is mainly about wording.
That is incomplete.
Better prompts do not begin with better phrasing.
They begin with better thinking.
A well-worded prompt without a position can still be weak.
A simple prompt with a clear position is often much stronger.
For example:

3.6.1 Weak

“Help me write about my project.”



3.6.2 Stronger

“I am building a guide about human-first AI use, and I want help structuring the argument so it is clear, practical, and defensible.”

The improvement comes from the position, not from stylistic complexity.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.7 Position Improves Ownership
Ownership matters because real work is not just about output.
It is about judgment.
If AI gives you language that you do not understand, you cannot explain it well.
If AI gives you a conclusion that you did not reason toward, you cannot defend it well.
That is why position matters.
It keeps you intellectually involved.
It keeps your thinking active.
It keeps responsibility where it belongs.

If AI gives you the idea, you may repeat it.
If you give AI the idea, you can refine and defend it.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.8 Position in Real Work
This principle applies across many kinds of work.

3.8.1 In analysis
A position may be:

“I want to test whether this pattern is real or just noise.”



3.8.2 In writing
A position may be:

“I want this explanation to be clear for beginners without losing rigor.”



3.8.3 In strategy
A position may be:

“I think this project needs to focus on reproducibility before expansion.”



3.8.4 In product work
A position may be:

“I want to evaluate whether this feature solves the actual user problem.”

In each case, the position creates direction before the tool is used.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
section: 3.9 Common Mistake: Confusing Curiosity with Position
text:
3.9 Common Mistake: Confusing Curiosity with Position
Curiosity is useful, but curiosity alone is not yet a position.
For example:

“I wonder what AI can do here.”
“Let me see what it says.”
“Maybe it has ideas.”

This is passive exploration.
By contrast, position-based use is active.
It says:

“Here is the problem.”
“Here is my current view.”
“Help me test, refine, or challenge it.”

That shift changes the quality of the interaction.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
section: 3.10 Common Mistake: Waiting for Perfect Clarity
text:
3.10 Common Mistake: Waiting for Perfect Clarity
Another mistake is waiting too long before engaging.
Some people delay because they think they must fully understand everything first.
But a position is not a final conclusion.
It is a starting point for disciplined reasoning.
You do not need certainty.
You need enough clarity to begin intentionally.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.11 A Practical Test
Before using AI, pause and ask:

What problem am I trying to solve?
What do I currently think?
What do I want AI to help me do?
What decision or output will still remain mine?

If you can answer these questions, even briefly, you likely have a position.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.12 From Position to Better Use
Once a position exists, AI can be used more effectively.
Instead of asking it to replace your thinking, you can ask it to:

challenge your reasoning
improve your structure
identify missing assumptions
compare alternatives
clarify communication
stress-test your approach

This is where AI becomes genuinely useful.
Not as a substitute for thought, but as support for it.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.13 Closing Insight
A position is not about sounding intelligent.
It is about beginning responsibly.
It gives your work direction.
It gives your prompts substance.
It gives your outputs meaning.
Most importantly, it keeps you connected to the reasoning process.

Do not start by asking AI what to think.
Start by stating where your thinking currently stands.

title: 3  What Is a Position?
text:
3.14 Key Takeaway
A position is your current thinking before using AI.
It may be a problem, a direction, or a hypothesis.
It does not need to be perfect.
It needs to be clear enough to guide the work.
That is where responsible AI use begins.

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.1 The Core Idea
Artificial intelligence is most effective when it is used within a structured reasoning process.
This process can be summarized as:
Human → AI → Human
This is not a technical pipeline.
It is a thinking framework.
AI should sit between two stages of human thinking:
This creates a loop, not a handoff.

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
You begin with direction

AI extends that direction

You return to evaluate and decide

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.2 Stage 1 — Human (Start with Thinking)
Before using AI, you define:

the problem

your position

your intent

This stage creates direction.
Without it, the interaction becomes unfocused.
Example:

“I want to test whether this pattern is meaningful or random.”

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.3 Stage 2 — AI (Extend Thinking)
AI supports your reasoning by:

expanding possibilities

organizing structure

identifying gaps

challenging assumptions

However, AI does not take responsibility for correctness.
It generates based on patterns, not accountability.

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.4 Stage 3 — Human (Evaluate and Decide)
You return to:

interpret the output

assess its validity

decide what to accept or reject

This is the most critical step.
It ensures:

understanding

ownership

defensibility

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.5 Why This Loop Matters
Without returning to the final human stage:

outputs remain unverified

reasoning remains incomplete

decisions become weak

With the loop:

thinking is extended, not replaced

outputs are examined critically

decisions remain grounded

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.6 Common Failure: Stopping at AI
Many workflows look like this:
Human → AI → Stop
This leads to:

copy-paste outputs

shallow understanding

poor decision quality

The missing step is evaluation.

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.7 Strengthening the Loop
To use this loop effectively:
After AI responds, ask:

Does this align with my position?
What assumptions are being made?
What is missing?
What would I change?

Then refine.

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.8 Loop, Not One-Time Use
This is not a single pass.
It can repeat:
Human → AI → Human → AI → Human
Each cycle improves:

clarity

structure

confidence

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.9 Real Example

4.9.1 Initial position

“I think this model may be overfitting.”



4.9.2 AI use
Ask for:

possible causes

diagnostic steps



4.9.3 Return to human

evaluate suggestions

test ideas

refine conclusion

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.10 Key Insight
AI is not the endpoint.
It is part of the process.

The value comes from how you move through the loop, not from the output alone.

title: 4  Human → AI → Human Loop
text:
4.11 Takeaway
Human → AI → Human is a reasoning loop.

You start with thinking

AI extends it

You return to evaluate and decide

This keeps:

direction with you

responsibility with you

understanding with you

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.1 The Core Shift
Prompting is often presented as a skill of wording.
But effective prompting is not primarily about wording.
It is about thinking.
Specifically, it is about starting from a position.
Most prompting advice focuses on:
These can help.
But they are secondary.
The primary driver of prompt quality is this:

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
how to phrase questions

how to structure instructions

how to use keywords





What you bring before you write the prompt.

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.2 Prompting Without a Position
When no position is defined, prompts tend to be vague.
Examples:

“Explain machine learning”

“Write a business plan”

“Analyze this dataset”

“Give me ideas”

These prompts:

lack direction

produce generic outputs

do not reflect your thinking

Even if the wording is improved, the result often remains shallow.

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.3 Prompting From a Position
A stronger approach is to start with:

a problem

a direction

or a hypothesis

Then use AI to extend that thinking.
Examples:

“I want to understand which model fits this problem and why.”

“I am building a small consulting service and need a realistic structure.”

“I want to test whether variable X is associated with outcome Y.”

“I have three possible directions. Help me compare them.”

These prompts:

carry intent

guide the response

produce more useful outputs

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
section: 5.4 The Structure of a Strong Prompt
text:
5.4 The Structure of a Strong Prompt
A strong prompt often contains:

Context What are you working on?
Position What do you currently think or want?
Task What should AI help you do?

Example:

“I am building a guide on human-first AI use.
My goal is to make it practical and defensible.
Help me structure the key sections clearly.”

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.5 Why This Works
AI responds to signals in the prompt.
A position provides strong signals:

what matters

what direction to follow

what level of detail is expected

Without these signals, AI defaults to general patterns.
With them, AI becomes more targeted.

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.6 Improving a Weak Prompt

5.6.1 Weak

“Help me write about AI.”



5.6.2 Improved

“I am writing a guide on responsible AI use and want to explain how to start with a position. Help me structure this clearly.”

The improvement comes from:

adding context

stating intent

defining direction

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.7 Prompting as a Thinking Process
Prompting should not be a one-step action.
It is part of the Human → AI → Human loop.
You may:

Write a prompt from your position

Review the output

Refine your prompt

Ask a more focused question

Each step improves clarity.

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.8 Common Mistake: Over-Engineering Prompts
Some users focus too much on:

complex instructions

long templates

rigid formats

These can help in specific cases.
But without a clear position, they do not solve the core problem.
A simple prompt with a strong position is often more effective than a complex prompt without one.

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.9 Practical Pattern
You can use this simple pattern:

“I am working on [context].
I think / I want [position].
Help me [task].”

Example:

“I am analyzing customer data.
I think churn may be driven by usage patterns.
Help me identify how to test this.”

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.10 Prompting for Different Goals

5.10.1 Exploration

“I am exploring possible causes of this pattern. What should I consider?”



5.10.2 Refinement

“Here is my current explanation. What is unclear or weak?”



5.10.3 Structuring

“Help me organize this into a clear workflow.”



5.10.4 Challenge

“Challenge this reasoning and identify gaps.”

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.11 Ownership Still Applies
Even with strong prompts, the responsibility remains yours.
AI can:

suggest

structure

expand

But it does not:

verify reality

take responsibility

make final decisions

That is your role.

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.12 Key Insight
Prompting is not about asking better questions alone.
It is about starting from better thinking.

Better prompts begin with a position.

title: 5  Prompting from a Position
text:
5.13 Takeaway

Do not start with empty requests

Start with a clear position

Use prompts to extend your thinking

This leads to:

more relevant outputs

clearer reasoning

stronger decisions

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.1 The Core Principle
AI can generate answers quickly.
Speed, however, is not reliability.
The critical skill is not only generating outputs, but interrogating them.
Interrogation means actively evaluating:

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
Do not accept AI output at face value. Examine it.



what the output says

how it is structured

what it assumes

what it omits

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.2 Why Interrogation Is Necessary
AI does not:

verify truth in real time

understand your full context

take responsibility for decisions

It generates plausible responses based on patterns.
This means outputs can be:

partially correct

incomplete

misleading

overconfident

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.3 The Risk of Passive Use
If you simply read and accept:

errors go unnoticed

assumptions remain hidden

weak reasoning passes through

This leads to:

poor analysis

weak communication

indefensible conclusions

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.4 The CDI Approach
Interrogation is part of interpretation discipline.
You move from:
Output → Examination → Understanding → Decision

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.5 Key Questions to Ask
After receiving an AI response, ask:

6.5.1 What is being claimed?

What is the main point?
Is it descriptive, explanatory, or prescriptive?




6.5.2 What assumptions are present?

What is taken for granted?
Are there hidden conditions?




6.5.3 What is missing?

Are alternative explanations considered?
Is context ignored?




6.5.4 Is this aligned with my position?

Does it address your actual problem?
Or has the direction drifted?




6.5.5 Can I explain this myself?

Do you understand it well enough to restate it?
Would you defend it if questioned?

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.6 Example

6.6.1 AI Output
“Feature X strongly predicts outcome Y.”


6.6.2 Interrogation

What does “strongly” mean?
Is this correlation or causation?
What data conditions apply?
Are there confounders?

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.7 Strengthening the Output
Interrogation is not only critical.
It is constructive.
You can ask AI to improve its own response:

“What assumptions did you make?”

“What could make this incorrect?”

“What alternative explanations exist?”

“Clarify this step more precisely.”

This turns AI into a refinement tool.

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.8 Avoiding Overconfidence
AI outputs often sound confident.
Confidence is not evidence.
A well-written response can still be:

incomplete

incorrect

misapplied

Your role is to separate clarity from correctness.

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.9 Iterative Interrogation
This process may repeat:

Receive output

Question it

Refine the prompt

Receive improved output

Each iteration improves:

clarity

depth

reliability

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
section: 6.10 Common Mistake: Accepting Fluency as Accuracy
text:
6.10 Common Mistake: Accepting Fluency as Accuracy
Fluent language can create false trust.
A clear explanation does not guarantee:

correctness

completeness

applicability

Always separate:

how it sounds

from what it actually means

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.11 From Output to Understanding
Interrogation bridges the gap between:

reading an answer

understanding a concept

Without interrogation, outputs remain external.
With interrogation, they become internalized.

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.12 Key Insight
AI gives you answers.
Interrogation turns them into understanding.

The value is not in receiving the output, but in examining it.

title: 6  Interrogating AI Outputs
text:
6.13 Takeaway

Do not accept outputs passively

Question assumptions and gaps

Align results with your position

Refine until you understand

This is how outputs become:

meaningful

reliable

defensible

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.1 The Core Principle
AI can generate useful outputs.
But usefulness is not enough.
In real work, outputs must be defensible.
Defensibility means:

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
An output is only valuable if you can explain and justify it.



you understand the reasoning

you can explain it clearly

you can justify it under questioning

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.2 From Output to Defensible Work
AI gives you:
Output
You must transform it into:
Understanding → Justification → Decision

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.3 What Makes an Output Defensible?
An output becomes defensible when you can:

7.3.1 Explain It

What does it mean?
How does it work?




7.3.2 Justify It

Why is it valid?
What supports it?




7.3.3 Bound It

When does it apply?
When might it fail?




7.3.4 Own It

Would you stand by it in a discussion?
Can you defend it without AI?

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.4 The Risk of Non-Defensible Outputs
Using outputs you cannot defend leads to:

weak reports

incorrect conclusions

loss of credibility

This is especially critical in:

analysis

research

business decisions

system design

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.5 The CDI Approach
CDI emphasizes:

From outputs to defensible decisions

This requires moving beyond:

copying results

repeating explanations

Into:

understanding

evaluation

justification

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.6 Turning Output into Understanding
Start by rewriting the output:

in your own words

in simpler terms

in a way you can explain to someone else

If you cannot do this, you do not yet understand it.

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.7 Testing Defensibility
Ask yourself:

Can I explain this without reading from the output?
What evidence supports this?
What assumptions are involved?
What would challenge this conclusion?

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.8 Example

7.8.1 AI Output
“This model performs well based on accuracy.”


7.8.2 Make It Defensible

What is “accuracy” measuring?
Is the dataset balanced?
Are there better metrics?
What are the limitations?

Now you move from:
Statement → Reasoned evaluation

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.9 Strengthening with AI
AI can help you improve defensibility if used correctly:

“What are the limitations of this conclusion?”
“Under what conditions would this fail?”
“What assumptions does this rely on?”
“How can this be justified more clearly?”

This keeps AI in a supporting role.

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.10 Common Mistake: Copying Without Ownership
Some users:

copy AI outputs directly

present them as final results

This creates:

fragile understanding

inability to answer questions

dependence on the tool

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.11 Defensibility and Responsibility
AI does not take responsibility.
You do.
That means:

you must validate

you must interpret

you must decide

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.12 From Defensible Output to Decision
Once an output is defensible:

you can act on it

you can communicate it

you can build on it

Without defensibility:

decisions are unstable

communication is weak

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.13 Key Insight
AI can generate answers.
Only you can make them defensible.

Defensibility transforms output into decision-ready work.

title: 7  Making Outputs Defensible
text:
7.14 Takeaway

Do not stop at output

Convert output into understanding

Test and justify your reasoning

Take ownership of decisions

This is how AI use becomes:

responsible

reliable

professional

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.1 The Core Principle
AI can generate outputs.
But real work requires more than outputs.
It requires claims.
This distinction is critical.

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text: An output is not a claim. A claim is an interpreted and justified statement.

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.2 What Is a Claim?
A claim is a statement you are willing to stand behind.
It is:

interpreted

contextualized

justified

Example:

8.2.1 Output
“Variable X is associated with outcome Y.”


8.2.2 Claim
“Variable X appears to be associated with outcome Y in this dataset, but this does not imply causation and may be influenced by confounding factors.”

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.3 Why This Matters
Outputs are:

raw

unexamined

incomplete

Claims are:

refined

bounded

defensible

Without this transformation:

conclusions become overstated

decisions become risky

communication becomes misleading

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.4 The Transformation Process
To move from output to claim:

8.4.1 Interpret

What does this actually mean?



8.4.2 Contextualize

Under what conditions does this apply?



8.4.3 Qualify

What are the limitations?



8.4.4 Justify

What supports this statement?

title: 8  From Output to Claims
section: 8.5 Common Mistake: Treating Output as Truth
text:
8.5 Common Mistake: Treating Output as Truth
AI outputs often sound complete.
But they are not final conclusions.
Mistakes include:

copying statements directly

ignoring assumptions

overstating certainty

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.6 Adding Qualification
Strong claims include boundaries.
Instead of: “This model is accurate.”
Say: “This model shows high accuracy on this dataset, but performance may vary depending on data distribution.”

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.7 Claim Calibration
Not all claims carry the same strength.
You should adjust:

confidence level

language precision

scope

Examples:

“suggests” instead of “proves”

“may indicate” instead of “demonstrates”

title: 8  From Output to Claims
section: 8.8 Role of AI in Claim Formation
text:
8.8 Role of AI in Claim Formation
AI can help you:

draft explanations

suggest interpretations

identify limitations

But it does not:

validate evidence

take responsibility

determine final claims

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.9 Testing Your Claim
Before finalizing, ask:

Can I explain this clearly?
What evidence supports it?
What would weaken it?
Am I overstating certainty?

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.10 Example

8.10.1 AI Output
“This feature improves performance.”


8.10.2 Defensible Claim
“This feature appears to improve performance in this dataset, but further validation is needed to confirm its effect across different conditions.”

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.11 Key Insight
Outputs inform.
Claims commit.

A claim is where responsibility begins.

title: 8  From Output to Claims
text:
8.12 Takeaway

Do not present outputs as conclusions

Interpret and qualify before stating claims

Adjust language to reflect evidence

Take responsibility for what you state

This is how work becomes:

credible

careful

defensible

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.1 The Core Principle
AI can be used well or poorly.
Many failures do not come from the tool itself.
They come from how it is used.
This chapter highlights common failure points and how to correct them.

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text: Most AI mistakes are thinking mistakes, not tool mistakes.

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.2 Failure 1 — Starting Without a Position

9.2.1 What it looks like

“Explain this”
“Give me ideas”
“What should I do?”



9.2.2 Problem

No direction
Generic outputs
Weak engagement



9.2.3 Correction
Start with: - a problem - a direction - a hypothesis

title: 9  Common Failure Points
section: 9.3 Failure 2 — Treating AI as an Authority
text:
9.3 Failure 2 — Treating AI as an Authority

9.3.1 What it looks like

Accepting outputs as correct
Not questioning results



9.3.2 Problem

Hidden errors
False confidence
Poor decisions



9.3.3 Correction
Interrogate outputs: - What assumptions? - What is missing? - Is this valid?

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.4 Failure 3 — Copy-Paste Without Understanding

9.4.1 What it looks like

Using outputs directly
Repeating explanations



9.4.2 Problem

No ownership
Cannot explain or defend
Fragile knowledge



9.4.3 Correction
Rewrite and explain: - in your own words - with clear understanding

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.5 Failure 4 — Confusing Fluency with Accuracy

9.5.1 What it looks like

Trusting well-written responses
Assuming clarity = correctness



9.5.2 Problem

Misleading conclusions
Overconfidence



9.5.3 Correction
Separate: - how it sounds - from what it means

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.6 Failure 5 — Over-Reliance on AI

9.6.1 What it looks like

Using AI for every step
Avoiding independent thinking



9.6.2 Problem

Reduced reasoning ability
Dependency on the tool



9.6.3 Correction
Use AI selectively: - for extension - not replacement

title: 9  Common Failure Points
section: 9.7 Failure 6 — Not Returning to Human Judgment
text:
9.7 Failure 6 — Not Returning to Human Judgment

9.7.1 What it looks like

Human → AI → Stop



9.7.2 Problem

No evaluation
No decision ownership



9.7.3 Correction
Complete the loop:
Human → AI → Human

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.8 Failure 7 — Overstating Claims

9.8.1 What it looks like

Presenting outputs as facts
Ignoring limitations



9.8.2 Problem

Misleading communication
Weak credibility



9.8.3 Correction
Calibrate claims: - add context - state limitations - avoid overstatement

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.9 Failure 8 — Over-Engineering Prompts

9.9.1 What it looks like

Complex templates
Long instructions without clarity



9.9.2 Problem

Adds complexity without direction



9.9.3 Correction
Focus on: - clear position - simple structure

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.10 Failure 9 — Ignoring Context

9.10.1 What it looks like

Generic prompts
Missing background information



9.10.2 Problem

Irrelevant outputs



9.10.3 Correction
Provide context: - what you are doing - why it matters

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.11 Failure 10 — Skipping Iteration

9.11.1 What it looks like

One prompt → final answer



9.11.2 Problem

Shallow results



9.11.3 Correction
Iterate: - refine prompts - question outputs - improve results

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.12 Recognizing Failure Early
Ask yourself:

Did I start with a position?
Do I understand this output?
Can I explain and defend it?
Have I questioned assumptions?

If not, revisit the process.

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.13 Key Insight
AI misuse is rarely about the tool.
It is about:

lack of direction

lack of questioning

lack of ownership


Good AI use is structured thinking.

title: 9  Common Failure Points
text:
9.14 Takeaway
Avoid:

passive use

blind trust

copy-paste behavior

Instead:

start with a position

interrogate outputs

make results defensible

This is how AI becomes:

useful

reliable

aligned with real work

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.1 The Core Principle
AI becomes valuable when it is used within real workflows.
This means integrating it into how work is actually done:
It fits into existing processes.
It does not eliminate the need for structure.

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text: AI should support workflows, not replace them.

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.2 Workflow 1 — Analysis

10.2.1 Typical Process

define the problem

explore data

test ideas

interpret results



10.2.2 Where AI Fits
Start with a position > “I want to test whether variable X explains outcome Y.”
Use AI to: - suggest analytical approaches
- identify potential pitfalls
- structure steps
Return to human - validate methods
- interpret results
- make conclusions

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.3 Workflow 2 — Writing

10.3.1 Typical Process

define message

structure content

draft

refine



10.3.2 Where AI Fits
Start with a position > “I want to explain this concept clearly for beginners.”
Use AI to: - outline structure
- improve clarity
- suggest phrasing
Return to human - ensure accuracy
- align tone
- take ownership of message

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.4 Workflow 3 — Decision-Making

10.4.1 Typical Process

define decision context

evaluate options

assess risks

decide



10.4.2 Where AI Fits
Start with a position > “I need to compare these two approaches and assess trade-offs.”
Use AI to: - list considerations
- identify risks
- compare scenarios
Return to human - weigh priorities
- consider context
- make final decision

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.5 Workflow 4 — System Building

10.5.1 Typical Process

define goal

design components

implement

test and refine



10.5.2 Where AI Fits
Start with a position > “I want to build a reproducible pipeline for this process.”
Use AI to: - outline architecture
- suggest implementation steps
- identify improvements
Return to human - validate design
- test system
- ensure reliability

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.6 The Consistent Pattern
Across all workflows:

Human defines direction
AI extends thinking
Human evaluates and decides

This is the same:
Human → AI → Human

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.7 What Changes Across Workflows
The role of AI varies:

In analysis → supports reasoning

In writing → supports communication

In decisions → supports evaluation

In systems → supports structure

But the responsibility remains the same.

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
section: 10.8 Common Mistake: Letting AI Lead the Workflow
text:
10.8 Common Mistake: Letting AI Lead the Workflow
Some users:

start with AI

follow its suggestions blindly

This leads to:

loss of direction

inconsistent results

weak outcomes

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.9 Maintaining Control
To keep control:

define your position clearly

guide the interaction

question outputs

validate before acting

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.10 Practical Integration
You do not need to redesign your workflow.
Instead:

insert AI at specific points

use it for support

keep core reasoning human-led

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.11 Key Insight
AI is not a separate workflow.
It is a component within your workflow.

Its value depends on how you integrate it, not how often you use it.

title: 10  AI in Real Workflows
text:
10.12 Takeaway

Use AI within structured workflows

Keep direction with you

Return to human judgment

This ensures:

consistency

reliability

defensibility

AI becomes most useful when it is:

integrated

controlled

aligned with real work

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.1 The Core Principle
Up to this point, you have learned how to:
The next step is to build your own workflow.
A workflow gives you:

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
Do not use AI randomly. Use it within a defined workflow.



consistency

clarity

control

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.2 What Is an AI Workflow?
An AI workflow is a structured way of working where:

each step has a purpose

AI is used intentionally

human judgment remains central

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.3 The CDI Workflow Model
You can use this simple structure:

11.3.1 Define Position

What is the problem?
What do you think?
What do you want to achieve?




11.3.2 Use AI to Extend

Generate ideas

Structure approach

Identify gaps




11.3.3 Interrogate Output

What assumptions are present?
What is missing?
Is it aligned with your position?




11.3.4 Make It Defensible

Rewrite in your own words

Justify reasoning

Add limitations




11.3.5 Decide and Act

Make a decision

Implement

Communicate

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.4 Visual Summary
Position → AI → Interrogate → Defensible → Decide

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.5 Adapting to Your Work
Your workflow may vary depending on your domain.

11.5.1 Analysis

Position → AI (methods) → Test → Interpret → Decide



11.5.2 Writing

Position → AI (structure) → Draft → Refine → Finalize



11.5.3 Decision-making

Position → AI (options) → Evaluate → Decide

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.6 Keep It Simple
A workflow does not need to be complex.
Even this is enough:

Start with a position

Use AI

Question the output

Make it your own

Act

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.7 Iteration Matters
Your workflow is not static.
It improves over time.
After each use, reflect:

What worked?
What was unclear?
What can be improved?

Then adjust.

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.8 Common Mistake: No Workflow
Without a workflow:

AI is used inconsistently

results vary

reasoning becomes fragmented

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.9 Common Mistake: Over-Complex Workflow
Too many steps can:

slow you down

reduce clarity

Balance is important.

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.10 Personalizing Your Workflow
Your workflow should reflect:

your type of work

your level of expertise

your goals

It should be:

practical

repeatable

adaptable

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.11 Key Insight
A workflow turns:

random interaction

into:

structured reasoning


Consistency in process leads to consistency in results.

title: 11  Building Your AI Workflow
text:
11.12 Takeaway

Build a simple, structured workflow

Keep AI within that workflow

Maintain human control

Improve over time

This is how AI use becomes:

systematic

reliable

aligned with real work

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.1 What You Have Learned
This guide introduced a different way to use AI.
Not as a tool that replaces thinking.
But as a system that extends thinking.
Across this guide, a consistent pattern emerged.

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.1.1 Start with a Position

Define your problem

State your thinking

Set direction




12.1.2 Use AI to Extend Thinking

Expand ideas

Structure workflows

Challenge assumptions




12.1.3 Interrogate Outputs

Question claims

Identify assumptions

Look for gaps




12.1.4 Make Outputs Defensible

Understand clearly

Justify reasoning

Define limitations




12.1.5 Move from Outputs to Claims

Interpret carefully

Calibrate language

Take responsibility




12.1.6 Apply in Real Workflows

Analysis

Writing

Decision-making

System building




12.1.7 Build Your Own Workflow

Keep it simple

Keep it consistent

Improve over time

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.2 The Complete CDI AI Model
You can summarize the entire guide as:
Position → AI → Interrogate → Defensible → Decide
This is not just a process.
It is a way of thinking.

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.3 What This Changes
Instead of:

asking for answers

copying outputs

relying on the tool

You now:

define direction

guide the interaction

evaluate results

make decisions

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.4 The Responsibility Remains Yours
AI does not:

understand consequences

validate reality

take responsibility

You do.
This is why:

AI does not replace thinking. It amplifies it.

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.5 From Learning to Practice
Understanding this framework is only the first step.
The real value comes from applying it consistently.
Start with small steps:

use a position in your next task

question the next AI output you receive

rewrite and explain what you use

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.6 Developing Skill Over Time
Like any skill, this improves with practice.
You will become better at:

forming clear positions

asking focused prompts

identifying weak reasoning

making stronger claims

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.7 Where This Leads
This approach prepares you for:

deeper analysis

clearer communication

better decision-making

building real systems

It aligns with how work is done in practice.

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.8 Final Insight
AI is powerful.
But its value depends on how it is used.

Clear thinking leads to better AI use.
Better AI use leads to better decisions.

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.9 Closing Takeaway

Start with thinking

Use AI intentionally

Question outputs

Make results defensible

Take ownership of decisions

This is how AI becomes:

useful

reliable

aligned with real work

title: 12  Summary and Next Steps
text:
12.10 Next Step
Apply this framework in your own work.
Do not aim for perfection.
Aim for:

clarity

consistency

improvement

That is where real progress happens.
